[{"title": "Openclaw AI Statistics: Market Data Report 2026", "url": "https://worldmetrics.org/openclaw-ai-statistics/", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Foundedin 2022byex-Googleengineers, Openclaw AI\u2014based in San Francisco with 150 full-time employees as of 2024 and ledbyStanford AI PhD Dr.", "relevance_score": 0.95}, {"title": "Web Design & Digital Marketing Services In Oxfordshire", "url": "https://akoca-seo.co.uk/", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Get found online through effective web design and transparent digitalmarketing, grow your revenue and succeed in the digital landscape.", "relevance_score": 0.6}, {"title": "The Do's and Don'ts of Using AI Tools for Marketing", "url": "https://sureoak.com/insights/ai-tools-for-marketing", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Machine learning inmarketinguses algorithms andstatisticalmodels to analyze large datasets and extract meaningful insights that can inform ...", "relevance_score": 0.6}, {"title": "AI Hallucination Statistics: Research Report 2026 - Suprmind", "url": "https://suprmind.ai/hub/insights/ai-hallucination-statistics-research-report-2026/", "snippet": "[Jina Parallel Content] Title: AI Hallucination Statistics: Research Report 2026\nURL Source: https://suprmind.ai/hub/insights/ai-hallucination-statistics-research-report-2026/\nPublished Time: 2026-02-15T22:20:13+00:00\nMarkdown Content:\n## Executive Overview\nAI hallucinations \u2013 instances where models generate false or fabricated information with full confidence \u2013 represent one of the most critical yet underappreciated risks in today\u2019s AI-powered business landscape. The data below makes the scale clear. What it also makes clear is that no model is immune, which is why [hallucination mitigation through multi-model verification](https://suprmind.ai/hub/ai-hallucination-mitigation/?utm_source=hallucinations_blog&utm_medium=intro_paragraph&utm_campaign=internal_link) is becoming a structural requirement, not an optional safeguard. \nThis report compiles raw statistical data from multiple authoritative benchmarks, industry studies, and real-world incident tracking to serve as a content foundation.\n**The headline numbers are staggering:**\n*   Global business losses from AI hallucinations reached **$67.4 billion in 2024** alone[1][2]\n*   **47% of business executives** have made major decisions based on unverified AI-generated content[3][1]\n*   Even the best AI models still hallucinate at least **0.7% of the time** on basic summarization tasks \u2014 and rates skyrocket to **18.7% on legal questions** and **15.6% on medical queries**[4]\n*   On difficult knowledge questions, **all but three out of 40 tested models** are more likely to hallucinate than give a correct answer[5][6]\n## What Is an AI Hallucination? (Technical Definition + Plain English)\n### Plain English\nAn AI hallucination happens when an AI model confidently makes something up. It doesn\u2019t say \u201cI don\u2019t know\u201d \u2014 it presents fabricated facts, invented statistics, fake legal cases, or nonexistent medical studies as if they were real. The response sounds authoritative and reads perfectly. That\u2019s what makes it dangerous.[7]\n### Technical Definition\nIn technical terms, hallucination refers to generated output that is **not grounded in the provided input data or factual reality**. There are two primary types:\n*   **Intrinsic hallucination** (also called \u201cfaithfulness hallucination\u201d): The model contradicts information explicitly provided in its source material. For example, during summarization, it adds facts not present in the original document.[8]\n*   **Extrinsic hallucination** (also called \u201cfactuality hallucination\u201d): The model generates information that cannot be verified against any known source \u2014 it invents facts, citations, statistics, or events from scratch.[9]\nA critical technical insight from MIT research (January 2025): when AI models hallucinate, they tend to use **more confident language than when providing factual information**. Models were **34% more likely** to use phrases like \u201cdefinitely,\u201d \u201ccertainly,\u201d and \u201cwithout doubt\u201d when generating incorrect information.[4]\nThis is the core paradox: the more wrong the AI is,", "relevance_score": 0.85}, {"title": "AI's Fact-Checking Failures: Why You Shouldn't Trust AI Search", "url": "https://admiralinnfalls.com/article/ai-s-fact-checking-failures-why-you-shouldn-t-trust-ai-search-engines", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Third , sinceverifyingeveryfactAI provides is necessary\u2014often more work than justcheckingyourself\u2014why bother? Using AI as a source may ...", "relevance_score": 0.5}, {"title": "Google", "url": "https://grokipedia.com/page/Google", "snippet": "[Jina Parallel Content] Title: Google URL Source: https://grokipedia.com/page/Google Markdown Content: ''For the number, see [Googol](https://grokipedia.com/page/Googol)''Google LLC is an American multinational technology company that specializes in internet-based services and products, including its search engine, video streaming platform YouTube, online advertising platforms, cloud computing, the [Android](https://grokipedia.com/page/Android_(operating_system)) mobile operating system, hardware, and artificial intelligence technologies. Founded in 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin as a Stanford University project employing the [PageRank](https://grokipedia.com/page/PageRank) algorithm to index and rank web pages, the company expanded into a broad digital ecosystem and was restructured in 2015 as a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc., with headquarters in Mountain View, California, and CEO Sundar Pichai since that year.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-1)[[1]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-1)Google's search engine handles billions of queries daily, capturing approximately 92% of global searches and generating most revenue from data-driven advertising auctions. Its portfolio encompasses the Chrome browser, YouTube, Google Cloud, Pixel smartphones, and Nest devices, alongside advancements in machine learning, mapping, translation, and autonomous driving through Waymo. ## History ### Founding and Early Innovations (1996\u20132003) [Larry Page](https://grokipedia.com/page/Larry_Page) and [Sergey Brin](https://grokipedia.com/page/Sergey_Brin), graduate students at Stanford University, began developing BackRub, a web search prototype, in early 1996 to study the mathematical properties of the World Wide Web's link structure.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-2)[[2]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-2) That year, they introduced PageRank, an algorithm that ranks webpages by the quantity and quality of incoming hyperlinks, treating links as endorsements of authority and relevance rather than relying on keyword matches used by prior engines.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-3)[[3]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-3) This network-based approach prioritized content quality through analysis, enabling more accurate results amid vast unstructured data.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-4)[[4]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-4)By August 1996, BackRub operated on Stanford's network, crawling and indexing pages to test PageRank.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-5)[[5]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-5) The project became Google, with google.com registered on September 15, 1997.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-6)[[6]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-6) Google Inc. incorporated in California on September 4, 1998, after Andy Bechtolsheim, Sun Microsystems co-founder, invested $100,000 to fund initial operations without revenue.[](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#ref-7)[[7]](https://grokipedia.com/page/Google#", "relevance_score": 0.85}]
